 {"id":"process_triage-a6q","title":"Code Organization & Standards","description":"## Overview
Code organization and standards for the `pt` bash wrapper and auxiliary scripts (installer, self-update, tool installation).

This epic exists to keep the wrapper:
- readable and maintainable,
- safe (no prompt hangs; clear error remediation),
- automation-friendly (semantic exit codes; stderr vs stdout discipline).

It is intentionally **not** about the premium TUI experience (that lives in `pt-core`).

## Standards
- Clear section markers for auditability.
- Semantic function naming (e.g., `cmd_*`, `ensure_*`, `log_*`).
- Stable exit codes and structured error messages with remediation hints.
- XDG-compliant paths for wrapper state where applicable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Wrapper scripts follow the standards above and pass ShellCheck.
- [ ] Exit codes and error messages are stable and documented.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Wrapper changes are safe to iterate on because tests + linting catch regressions.
- [ ] Operational failures (install/update/tooling) are debuggable from logs alone.

 {"id":"process_triage-agz","title":"Define capabilities cache schema","description":"## Task
Specify the schema for caching detected system capabilities and tool availability.

## Background
The wrapper (pt bash) performs capability discovery and passes results to pt-core. This enables:
- Core to make decisions based on available tools
- Graceful degradation when tools are missing
- User awareness of what's available vs missing
- Conditional probe selection

pt agent capabilities returns:
- Platform info (OS, kernel, container status)
- Data sources (procfs, sysfs, perf, eBPF, etc.)
- Supervisors detected (systemd, launchd, pm2, Docker)
- Available actions (kill, pause, renice, cgroup ops)
- Permission level (own user, sudo, root)
- Resource limits (overhead budget, probe limits)
- Feature flags (galaxy-brain, community signatures)

## Deliverables
- JSON schema for capabilities cache
- Detection protocol for each capability
- Version information for detected tools
- Safe fallbacks when tools missing
- Refresh/invalidation strategy
- Per-action capability requirements

## Technical Considerations
- Cache location: ~/.cache/pt/capabilities.json
- Include detection timestamp for staleness
- Some capabilities are runtime-dependent (sudo may work sometimes)
- Container detection affects many capabilities
- macOS SIP affects tracing capabilities

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-aii","title":"EPIC: Testing Infrastructure and Quality","description":"## Overview
EPIC for comprehensive testing infrastructure ensuring correctness and reliability across all components.

## Background
The plan specifies rigorous testing for a system that makes kill decisions. Testing is critical because errors could destroy user work. This EPIC covers unit tests, integration tests, property tests, and benchmarks.

## Why It Matters
Process triage decisions are irreversible. A false positive kills a useful process; user data may be lost. Testing verifies correctness of the math, evidence collection, and decision logic before any process is harmed.

## Scope
1. Unit tests for all pure functions (math, parsing)
2. Integration tests for evidence collection
3. Property-based tests for Bayesian inference
4. End-to-end tests for CLI workflows
5. Performance benchmarks with regression detection
6. Mock infrastructure for isolated testing

## Testing Principles
- Fast: Unit tests run in seconds
- Isolated: No dependency on real processes for unit tests
- Comprehensive: Cover edge cases and error paths
- Reproducible: Deterministic results, seeded randomness
- Documented: Tests serve as executable specification

## Coverage Targets
- Math utilities: 100% line coverage
- Evidence collection: 90% line coverage
- Inference engine: 95% branch coverage
- CLI: All commands and flag combinations

## CI Integration
- All tests run on every PR
- Benchmark comparison against main branch
- Coverage reports with trend tracking
- Mutation testing for test quality

## Success Criteria
- All tests pass reliably
- Coverage targets met
- Benchmarks detect regressions
- Testing enables confident refactoring

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

-{"id":"process_triage-aii.1","title":"Implement E2E action-tray tests (pause/throttle/renice/cgroups) with detailed logging","description":"## Overview
Add end-to-end tests specifically for the **expanded action space** (Plan §6) and the **staged execution protocol** (observe/mitigate before kill), with strong, structured logging to diagnose failures.

These tests complement the general CLI E2E bead by focusing on the highest-risk integration surface: action planning + policy gating + execution + verification.

## Scenarios (must be covered)
### 1) Pause → observe → resume
- Spawn a parent+child workload
- Apply pause to PGID
- Verify CPU drops and process remains present
- Resume and verify recovery

### 2) Throttle (cgroup CPU) → verify relief
- In an isolated test cgroup, throttle CPU
- Verify CPU usage drops (within tolerance)
- Restore previous settings

### 3) Renice → verify scheduling change
- Renice a CPU burner
- Verify nice value changes and is logged

### 4) Kill staged escalation
- SIGTERM with grace period
- If still alive, SIGKILL
- Verify termination and no PID-reuse mixups

### 5) Safety gates (robot mode)
- Ensure robot mode refuses actions when:
  - protected patterns match
  - data-loss gate triggers
  - identity revalidation fails
  - blast-radius caps exceeded

## Logging requirements
- Capture:
  - the generated plan (JSON)
  - every action attempt (structured)
  - verification results
  - failure recovery hints
- On failure, print a minimal replay bundle reference (session artifact dir) to aid debugging.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tests cover the scenarios above on supported CI environments (or skip with explicit capability checks).
- [ ] Failures produce actionable logs (plan + action outcomes + verification).
- [ ] Tests are deterministic (bounded timeouts, controlled fixtures).

## Test Plan
- E2E: run in CI with capability detection (skip cgroup tests when unsupported).
- Local: provide a script to run these tests with verbose logging.

 {"id":"process_triage-aii.2","title":"E2E: .ptb bundle export/import integrity tests (profiles, checksums, redaction)","description":"## Purpose
Add end-to-end tests that prove `.ptb` bundles are:
- portable
- integrity-checked (manifest + checksums)
- redaction-safe
- reproducible (importing the bundle recreates the same session summary/report surface)

These tests are intentionally **black-box** at the CLI boundary: they validate what users/agents actually rely on.

## Plan requirements covered
From Plan §3.6 + §11:
- `.ptb` is a single-file export container (ZIP default) with:
  - `manifest.json` (schema versions, tool versions/capabilities, host fingerprint, redaction policy version, checksums)
  - `plan.json`
  - telemetry partitions (profile-dependent)
  - optional `raw/` (capped + redacted)
  - optional `report.html` (single file)
- Export profiles:
  - `minimal` (plan + summary only)
  - `safe` (derived features/inference; aggressive redaction)
  - `forensic` (more raw; still policy-redacted; optional encryption handled separately)
- Import must validate checksums and **fail closed** on mismatch.

## Test scope
### 1) Round-trip correctness by profile
For each profile (`minimal`, `safe`, `forensic`):
- Generate a fixture session.
- `pt agent export --profile <profile> --out fixture.ptb`.
- Re-import/read the bundle (via the bundle reader or `pt agent show/report` path).
- Assert:
  - `manifest.json` exists and includes required version fields.
  - `plan.json` exists and matches the original plan’s stable fields (session_id, candidate identities, recommended actions).
  - The report surface renders (either embedded report.html or generated from bundle contents) without missing required fields.

### 2) Integrity failure (tamper tests)
- Flip a byte in the bundle.
- Assert import fails with a deterministic error code and an explicit “checksum mismatch” classification.

### 3) Redaction safety regression tests
Using fixtures containing known sensitive strings:
- Ensure secrets/PII **do not appear** in:
  - `plan.json`
  - `manifest.json`
  - `report.html` (if included)
  - any embedded raw artifacts for `safe`

### 4) Size/overhead sanity
- Large fixture (500–1000 synthetic processes) exports within reasonable size bounds.
- Export time is logged and bounded (regression alert when it grows).

## Fixtures
- A small deterministic fixture with a mix of:
  - orphan-with-supervision
  - zombie remediation case
  - a high-blast-radius server process (to ensure the bundle contains risk metadata)
- A large deterministic fixture for performance.

## Logging requirements (tests)
On failure, tests must log:
- profile
- bundle size
- checksum list and which checksum failed
- redaction scan hits (matched patterns)
- import error classification and stderr

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Round-trip export/import works for `minimal`, `safe`, and `forensic` profiles.
- [ ] Tampered bundles fail closed with clear error classification.
- [ ] Redaction invariants hold across bundle contents.
- [ ] Size/time metrics are recorded for regression tracking.

## Test Plan
- E2E: CLI round-trips and tamper tests.
- Property-ish: generate multiple fixtures with randomized sensitive strings and assert redaction.

 {"id":"process_triage-aii.3","title":"E2E: Dormant daemon (ptd) tests (overhead, triggers, cooldown, inbox)","description":"## Purpose
Add end-to-end tests for **dormant mode** (always-on guardian) so we can trust:
- it stays low-overhead,
- it triggers when it should,
- it does not spam/loop,
- it produces the correct artifacts (session + inbox entry), and
- it never violates safety defaults.

This is explicitly required by Plan §11 (“Dormant daemon tests: low overhead, trigger correctness, cooldown/backoff behavior, escalation produces a session + inbox entry”).

## Background (Plan §3.7 + §10 Phase 8/daemon-related + §11)
Dormant mode is special because it runs continuously; a bug here can degrade the user’s machine or flood them with noise. Tests must therefore validate both correctness **and** resource/operational behavior.

## Scope
### What we are testing
1. **Overhead budget**
   - CPU and memory usage of daemon loop under idle conditions.
   - No unbounded log/event growth.

2. **Trigger correctness**
   - When sustained evidence thresholds are met, daemon escalates to create a triage session.
   - Trigger windows are robust to transient spikes (EWMA + sustained windows).

3. **Cooldown / backoff**
   - After a trigger, cooldown prevents repeated sessions for the same condition.
   - Failure paths use backoff (no tight crash loops).

4. **Artifact + inbox correctness**
   - A trigger produces:
     - durable `session_id`
     - artifact directory (plan + samples + derived + telemetry pointers)
     - inbox item that is actionable by humans/agents

5. **Coordination invariants**
   - Respects per-user `pt lock` (does not race manual runs / agent runs).
   - Does not compete for heavy probes when lock is held (queues inbox instead).

6. **Safety invariants**
   - Dormant mode is advisory-only by default (no auto-kill).
   - Any “suggested” actions are gated by policy and do not execute.

## Test Harness Requirements
- Provide a **test mode** for daemon execution:
  - controllable time source (fake clock) to test cooldown/backoff deterministically
  - deterministic fixture inputs for trigger conditions
  - explicit “single tick” execution for unit-style control

- Provide process fixtures:
  - synthetic process set generator OR controlled lightweight fixture processes
  - ability to simulate signals (CPU deltas, memory growth, orphaning) without requiring privileged ops

## Concrete Test Cases (minimum)
- `test_ptd_idle_low_overhead`
  - Run daemon in idle mode for N ticks; assert CPU/mem within budget; assert no unbounded file growth.

- `test_ptd_trigger_creates_session_and_inbox`
  - Feed sustained trigger evidence; assert session artifacts created; inbox item produced with correct session_id.

- `test_ptd_cooldown_prevents_repeat_sessions`
  - Trigger once; continue feeding evidence; verify no second session until cooldown expires.

- `test_ptd_backoff_on_failure`
  - Force an internal failure (simulated tool timeout / write error); assert backoff increases; no tight loop.

- `test_ptd_respects_pt_lock`
  - Hold the lock (simulate active manual run); feed trigger evidence; daemon must queue inbox item (or defer) without performing heavy work.

- `test_ptd_escalation_ladder_notifications`
  - Validate notification/escalation ladder behavior (quiet → notify → high urgency), including dedupe.

## Logging Requirements (tests)
- Every test prints a structured “why” block on failure:
  - trigger threshold inputs, computed EWMA/window state
  - cooldown/backoff counters
  - session_id + artifact paths
  - inbox payload summary
  - measured overhead metrics (CPU %, RSS, IO bytes)

- Capture daemon logs and assert presence of:
  - `ptd.tick.start/end`
  - `ptd.trigger.evaluated`
  - `ptd.trigger.fired`
  - `ptd.cooldown.active`
  - `ptd.inbox.enqueued`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All tests above exist and pass reliably.
- [ ] Tests are deterministic (fake clock / fixed fixtures) and do not require root.
- [ ] Logs on failure are sufficient to diagnose “why did it trigger?” or “why didn’t it trigger?”

## Dependencies
- Dormant daemon epic: `process_triage-b4v`
- Daemon core loop: `process_triage-nh7p`
- Inbox command surface: `process_triage-iqe`
- Session model/artifacts: `process_triage-qje`
- Escalation/notifications: `process_triage-a3h0`, `process_triage-1k6`
","notes":"Progress (SapphireSpring - 2026-02-06)
- Added daemon E2E integration tests (feature-gated) in `crates/pt-core/tests/e2e_daemon_dormant.rs`:
  - Lock contention writes `inbox/items.jsonl` (type=lock_contention) when `.pt-lock` is held.
  - Trigger cooldown prevents repeated lock-contention spam across ticks.
  - Overhead budget exceeded is persisted in `PROCESS_TRIAGE_DATA/daemon/state.json` and skips escalation/inbox writes.

- CI wiring: `cargo_test` job (Linux) now runs daemon tests via:
  - cargo test -p pt-core --verbose --features daemon

Quality gates
- cargo test -p pt-core --features daemon --test e2e_daemon_dormant

Progress (FuchsiaBeaver - 2026-02-06)
- Daemon now always writes a dormant_escalation inbox item on completed escalation (even if candidates_found == 0).

 {"id":"process_triage-aii.4","title":"Tests: Galaxy-brain math ledger consistency (TUI/agent/report)","description":"## Purpose
Add tests that guarantee the **galaxy‑brain math ledger** is:
- internally consistent (equations ↔ substituted numbers ↔ computed outputs),
- consistent across surfaces (TUI, `pt agent explain`, HTML report), and
- safe to share (redaction applied; no secrets leak).

This is explicitly required by Plan §11 (“Galaxy-brain mode tests: math ledger includes equations + concrete numbers and matches the underlying inference outputs.”).

## Background (Plan §7.8 + §11)
Galaxy‑brain mode is a trust feature: it must be correct, reproducible, and auditable. A mismatch between displayed math and actual decisions destroys trust.

## Scope
### 1) Ledger content correctness
For a deterministic fixture session, assert ledger includes (at minimum):
- Posterior by class `P(C|x)` and log-odds
- Bayes factors / evidence strengths for top features
- Expected-loss table and chosen action
- FDR/e‑FDR selection summary (k chosen, α used, membership)
- Alpha‑investing wealth state (if enabled)
- VOI computations for next best probe (if enabled)

### 2) Substituted-number correctness
- Each “math card” includes:
  - equation template
  - substituted numeric values
  - resulting numeric output
- Test asserts substituted numbers match the actual numeric fields used in computation.

### 3) Cross-surface consistency
Given the same session fixture:
- `pt agent explain --galaxy-brain` output matches the ledger stored in session artifacts.
- HTML report “Galaxy‑Brain” tab matches the same ledger data (modulo formatting).
- TUI drilldown (if snapshot-testable) presents the same key values.

### 4) Redaction safety
- Ledger never contains raw sensitive strings.
- Any command lines / paths / env var evidence shown in galaxy-brain mode must use redacted/hashes.

## Suggested Test Strategy
- Unit tests:
  - validate ledger JSON schema and required fields
  - validate card-by-card numeric equality within tolerances

- Golden tests:
  - fixture session inputs → exact expected ledger outputs (stable formatting)

- Snapshot tests:
  - report HTML contains a Galaxy‑Brain tab and embeds the ledger (or references embedded JSON) correctly

## Concrete Test Cases (minimum)
- `test_galaxy_brain_ledger_schema_and_required_cards`
- `test_galaxy_brain_posterior_numbers_match_inference`
- `test_galaxy_brain_expected_loss_numbers_match_decision`
- `test_galaxy_brain_fdr_selection_matches_decision_output`
- `test_galaxy_brain_report_tab_renders_and_contains_ledger`
- `test_galaxy_brain_redaction_no_secrets_in_ledger`

## Logging Requirements (tests)
- On failure, print:
  - the ledger card ID
  - expected numeric values and actual numeric values
  - the source-of-truth struct/field name used to compute the value
  - a compact diff of ledger JSON

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Ledger math tests pass deterministically on fixtures.
- [ ] Cross-surface consistency is verified (agent + report at minimum).
- [ ] Redaction invariants are verified by scanning outputs.

## Dependencies
- Evidence ledger generation: `process_triage-myq`
- Posterior computation: `process_triage-e48`
- Bayes factors: `process_triage-0ij`
- Decision/expected loss: `process_triage-d88`
- FDR selection: `process_triage-sqe`
- Alpha-investing: `process_triage-cpm`
- VOI: `process_triage-brh7`
- Agent explain surface: `process_triage-e3qe`
- Galaxy-brain UI display: `process_triage-wme`, `process_triage-8gfb`
- HTML report generator (galaxy-brain tab): `process_triage-k4yc.5`

- Should coordinate with existing E2E tasks in Testing Infrastructure epic.

 {"id":"process_triage-aii.8","title":"Implement fuzz testing for proc parsers and config loading","description":"## Overview
Add fuzz testing for security-critical parsing code, particularly /proc file parsers and config loaders.

## Background
Process triage parses untrusted input from /proc filesystem and user config files. Fuzz testing catches edge cases that could cause panics, hangs, or incorrect behavior.

## Scope

### 1. Target Modules
- \`collect/proc_parsers.rs\`: /proc/stat, /proc/statm, /proc/io, etc.
- \`collect/network.rs\`: /proc/net/tcp, /proc/net/unix parsing
- \`config/priors.rs\`: priors.json parsing
- \`config/policy.rs\`: policy.json parsing
- \`redact/detect.rs\`: Secret detection regex
- \`bundle/reader.rs\`: .ptb bundle parsing

### 2. Fuzz Harness Setup
- Use cargo-fuzz with libfuzzer (primary)
- AFL++ integration as alternative
- Each target module has dedicated harness
- Harnesses in \`fuzz/fuzz_targets/\`

### 3. Corpus Collection
- Sample real /proc outputs from various systems (Ubuntu, Fedora, Alpine, Arch)
- Edge case configs (empty, huge, malformed JSON)
- Known problematic inputs from bug reports
- Generated corner cases (max values, special characters)
- Store in \`fuzz/corpus/\`

### 4. Coverage Goals
- All pub fn that parse strings/bytes
- Input validation paths
- Error handling paths
- UTF-8 edge cases
- Integer overflow scenarios

### 5. CI Integration
- OSS-Fuzz integration for continuous fuzzing
- CI runs fuzz tests (time-limited: 60s per target)
- Crash reproduction in CI
- Corpus synced from OSS-Fuzz

---

## Testing Requirements

### Fuzz Targets
- **File**: \`fuzz/fuzz_targets/proc_stat.rs\`
  - Target: parse_proc_stat, parse_proc_statm
  - Input: arbitrary bytes simulating /proc content
  - Assertion: no panic, no hang (timeout 10s)

- **File**: \`fuzz/fuzz_targets/proc_io.rs\`
  - Target: parse_proc_io
  - Input: arbitrary /proc/PID/io content
  - Assertion: returns Ok or Err, never panic

- **File**: \`fuzz/fuzz_targets/config_priors.rs\`
  - Target: Priors::load
  - Input: arbitrary JSON bytes
  - Assertion: no panic, graceful error for invalid input

- **File**: \`fuzz/fuzz_targets/config_policy.rs\`
  - Target: Policy::load
  - Input: arbitrary JSON bytes
  - Assertion: graceful handling

- **File**: \`fuzz/fuzz_targets/network_parser.rs\`
  - Target: parse_tcp_connections, parse_unix_sockets
  - Input: /proc/net/* content
  - Assertion: no panic

- **File**: \`fuzz/fuzz_targets/bundle_reader.rs\`
  - Target: Bundle::read
  - Input: arbitrary bytes (potentially encrypted)
  - Assertion: graceful error for invalid bundles

### Crash Reproduction
- **File**: \`fuzz/reproduce/\`
- Each crash artifact saved with:
  - Input bytes that caused crash
  - Stack trace
  - Test case to reproduce
- Regression tests generated from crashes

### Coverage Tracking
- Use cargo-fuzz coverage mode
- Report coverage delta from fuzzing
- Track which functions hit by fuzz vs unit tests

---

## Logging Specifications

### Structured Log Events
| Event | Level | Fields | Purpose |
|-------|-------|--------|---------|
| \`fuzz.session_start\` | INFO | target, duration, corpus_size | Fuzz session begins |
| \`fuzz.crash_found\` | ERROR | target, input_hash, stack_trace | Crash detected |
| \`fuzz.corpus_grow\` | DEBUG | target, new_inputs | Corpus expanded |
| \`fuzz.coverage\` | INFO | target, lines_covered, branches_covered | Coverage report |
| \`fuzz.session_end\` | INFO | target, iterations, crashes_found | Session complete |

---

## Error Handling

### For Fuzzing Infrastructure
| Scenario | Detection | Recovery | Action |
|----------|-----------|----------|--------|
| Fuzz crash | Non-zero exit | Save artifact | Create regression test |
| Timeout | Process killed | Save input | Investigate for DoS |
| OOM | Memory limit | Save input | Investigate memory leak |
| Coverage plateau | No new paths | Reduce duration | Move on |

### For Target Code (what fuzzing should catch)
- Panic on malformed input → FIX to return error
- Hang on crafted input → FIX to add timeout/limit
- Memory exhaustion → FIX to add size limits
- Integer overflow → FIX to use checked arithmetic

---

## Performance Targets
- CI fuzz run: 60s per target (discovery mode)
- OSS-Fuzz: 24h continuous (deep mode)
- Corpus size: < 10MB per target
- No timeouts at 10s per input

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Fuzz harnesses for all proc_parsers
- [ ] Fuzz harnesses for config loading
- [ ] Fuzz harnesses for bundle reading
- [ ] CI runs fuzz tests (time-limited)
- [ ] OSS-Fuzz integration setup (or similar)
- [ ] No panics on any fuzz input (after fixes)
- [ ] Corpus committed to repo
- [ ] Regression tests for any crashes found
- [ ] Coverage report shows parsing paths hit

## Dependencies
- cargo-fuzz installation
- libfuzzer or AFL++
- Part of: Testing Infrastructure (process_triage-aii)","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CobaltBeaver","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T18:53:42.176941680Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-04T06:15:28.694997304Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-aii.8","depends_on_id":"process_triage-aii","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:57Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"process_triage-aii.9","title":"Implement chaos testing for daemon mode resilience","description":"## Overview
Implement chaos testing for daemon mode resilience under adverse conditions.

## Background
The dormant daemon (ptd) runs continuously and must handle failures gracefully: disk full, network partitions, OOM pressure, signal storms, etc.

## Scope

### 1. Fault Injection Framework
- Framework for injecting faults during E2E tests
- Faults: disk full, slow disk, network timeout, SIGTERM storm
- Controllable via environment variables or test API
- Clean recovery after test

### 2. Resource Exhaustion Tests
- **Disk full**: Fill temp partition, verify daemon handles write failures
- **Memory pressure**: Limit cgroup memory, verify graceful degradation
- **File descriptor exhaustion**: Limit FDs, verify daemon doesn't hang
- **CPU starvation**: Nice to 19 + CPU limit, verify continued operation

### 3. Signal Handling Tests
- SIGTERM: Clean shutdown within timeout
- SIGINT: Same as SIGTERM
- SIGHUP: Reload configuration
- SIGUSR1/2: Status dump / force scan
- Signal storm: 1000 signals in 1s, no crash

### 4. Recovery Tests
- Daemon restart after OOM kill: recovers state
- Daemon restart after SIGKILL: clean restart
- Config file deleted: graceful error
- Telemetry lake corrupted: rebuild or bypass

### 5. Network Partition Tests
- Fleet mode: Coordinator unreachable
- OTLP endpoint: Exporter unavailable
- GitHub API: Update check fails
- All network gone: Offline operation

### 6. Time-Related Tests
- Clock skew: System time jumps forward/backward
- NTP correction: Large time adjustment
- Leap second: (if applicable)
- Long uptime: 30+ day simulation

---

## Testing Requirements

### Chaos Test Suite
- **File**: \`test/chaos/daemon_chaos.bats\`
- Infrastructure: Docker with cgroups control
- Each test: setup → inject fault → verify behavior → cleanup

### Resource Exhaustion Tests
- **File**: \`test/chaos/resource_exhaustion.bats\`
- Test cases:
  - \`test_disk_full\`: Fill /tmp, verify daemon logs error, continues scan
  - \`test_memory_pressure\`: 50MB limit, verify no OOM on normal load
  - \`test_fd_exhaustion\`: 100 FD limit, verify graceful queuing
  - \`test_cpu_starvation\`: 5% CPU limit, verify scan completes (slowly)

### Signal Handling Tests
- **File**: \`test/chaos/signal_handling.bats\`
- Test cases:
  - \`test_sigterm_clean_shutdown\`: Exit within 5s, no data loss
  - \`test_sighup_reload\`: Config reloaded without restart
  - \`test_signal_storm\`: 1000 SIGUSR1 in 1s, no crash
  - \`test_sigkill_recovery\`: Restart recovers state

### Recovery Tests
- **File**: \`test/chaos/recovery.bats\`
- Test cases:
  - \`test_restart_after_oom\`: State recovered from persistence
  - \`test_corrupt_state\`: Daemon rebuilds from scratch
  - \`test_missing_config\`: Clear error, defaults used

### Network Partition Tests
- **File**: \`test/chaos/network_partition.bats\`
- Test cases (using network namespaces or iptables):
  - \`test_otlp_unreachable\`: Traces buffered locally
  - \`test_fleet_coordinator_down\`: Node operates standalone
  - \`test_github_unreachable\`: Update check skipped

### Long-Running Tests
- **File**: \`test/chaos/longevity.bats\`
- Test cases:
  - \`test_24h_operation\`: No memory leak, no FD leak
  - \`test_1000_scans\`: Performance doesn't degrade
  - \`test_clock_jump\`: Time-based logic handles discontinuity

---

## Logging Specifications

### Structured Log Events for Chaos
| Event | Level | Fields | Purpose |
|-------|-------|--------|---------|
| \`chaos.fault_inject\` | WARN | fault_type, parameters | Fault injected |
| \`chaos.fault_clear\` | INFO | fault_type | Fault cleared |
| \`chaos.recovery_start\` | INFO | from_state | Recovery begins |
| \`chaos.recovery_complete\` | INFO | duration_ms, data_loss | Recovery done |

### Daemon Resilience Logs
| Event | Level | Fields | Purpose |
|-------|-------|--------|---------|
| \`daemon.resource_warning\` | WARN | resource, current, limit | Approaching limit |
| \`daemon.resource_exhausted\` | ERROR | resource, action_taken | Limit hit |
| \`daemon.signal_received\` | INFO | signal, action | Signal handled |
| \`daemon.graceful_shutdown\` | INFO | reason, duration_ms | Clean exit |

---

## Error Handling

### Expected Daemon Behavior Under Chaos
| Fault | Expected Behavior |
|-------|------------------|
| Disk full | Log error, skip persistence, continue scan |
| OOM kill | Restart recovers state, no duplicate actions |
| Network down | Buffer telemetry, operate offline |
| Signal storm | Process signals, no crash, may drop excess |
| Clock jump | Adjust timers, log warning |
| Config deleted | Use defaults, log warning |

### Test Verification
- Daemon exits cleanly (exit code 0) on controlled faults
- No data corruption after any fault
- State recoverable after crash

---

## Performance Targets
- Graceful shutdown: < 5s
- Recovery after crash: < 10s to operational
- Resource overhead under pressure: < 2x normal

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Fault injection framework operational
- [ ] Resource exhaustion tests pass
- [ ] Signal handling tests pass
- [ ] Recovery tests pass
- [ ] Network partition tests pass
- [ ] 24h longevity test passes (no leaks)
- [ ] All chaos tests documented
- [ ] CI runs subset of chaos tests

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Dormant daemon (process_triage-b4v)

 {"id":"process_triage-aip","title":"Release Automation with Checksums","description":"## Overview
Automate releases so users can install/verify the project safely and easily.

This epic must support the plan’s two-layer architecture:
- `pt` (bash wrapper)
- `pt-core` (Rust monolithic binary)

## Release Trigger
- Git tag push: `vX.Y.Z`

## What a Release Must Produce
### Source-level artifacts
- `pt` wrapper script
- `install.sh`

### `pt-core` binaries (minimum initial matrix)
- Linux x86_64
- Linux aarch64
- macOS x86_64
- macOS aarch64

(Windows can be added later if/when supported.)

### Integrity artifacts
- `checksums.sha256` containing SHA-256 for every uploaded file
- Optional per-file `.sha256` files for installer-friendly verification

## Version Consistency
Releases must be internally consistent:
- Tag version matches:
  - the shared `VERSION` source of truth (see `process_triage-nk1`)
  - `pt-core --version`
  - wrapper-reported version (if wrapper exposes one)

CI should block mismatches so broken releases are impossible.

## Security / Supply Chain Considerations
- Checksums are generated in CI from the exact uploaded bytes.
- Release notes include verification instructions.
- Optional future upgrade: artifact signing (cosign/minisign) and SBOM generation.

## Success Criteria
- A tagged release is fully installable on supported platforms.
- Verification is straightforward (checksums) and documented.
- Releases are reproducible and don’t ship mismatched versions.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tag push creates a GitHub release with wrapper + installer + all `pt-core` binaries.
- [ ] Release fails fast on version mismatch.
- [ ] Checksums are generated and uploaded, and match downloaded artifacts.
- [ ] Install flow can verify `pt-core` via checksums (supports `VERIFY=1`).

## Notes
- Keep artifact naming conventions stable; installer logic should not chase moving targets.

 {"id":"process_triage-b9x","title":"Create bundled signature library (50+ processes)","description":"## Overview

The plan calls for bundled signatures covering common processes developers encounter. This provides immediate value out of the box and serves as examples for community contributions.

### Databases (5+)

### Containers/Orchestration (5+)

## Success Criteria

 {"id":"process_triage-bb96","title":"Implement coupled tree priors for hierarchical process classification","description":"## Section 4.30 - Coupled Tree Priors

**Purpose**: Share statistical strength across process types via tree-structured priors. 'jest tests' and 'vitest tests' should pool information since they're both JS test runners.

**Mathematical Background**:
- Tree-structured prior: θ_leaf ~ N(θ_parent, σ²_level)
- Hierarchical shrinkage: Leaf estimates shrunk toward ancestors
- Polya tree: Recursive partitioning with Beta-distributed split probabilities
- Mondrian process: Random axis-aligned partitions with exponential split times
- Cascade prior: p(θ_child | θ_parent) propagates down the tree

**Implementation Requirements**:
1. `build_type_tree(process_types)` - Hierarchy: test → js_test → jest
2. `tree_prior(tree, base_params)` - Generate coupled priors
3. `tree_posterior(tree, data)` - Update all nodes given leaf observations
4. `shrinkage_estimator(observation, ancestors)` - Empirical Bayes shrinkage

**Why This Matters for pt**:
Few 'vitest' kills in history, but many 'test runner' kills. Tree prior borrows strength: vitest posterior is informed by all test runner data, not just vitest.

**Integration Points**:
- Process classification (Section 2.2)
- Prior elicitation (Section 2.3)
- Empirical Bayes (Section 4.34)

**Test Requirements**:
- Verify shrinkage toward root with little leaf data
- Verify shrinkage decreases with more leaf data
- Compare tree prior vs independent priors on sparse data","notes":"Duplicate/out-of-tree bead.  {"id":"process_triage-bfiq","title":"Test infra: automation_mode tests timeout due to real system inference","description":"## Problem
The automation_mode tests in `crates/pt-core/tests/automation_mode.rs` are failing due to timeouts. Tests have a 120-second timeout but real system inference takes much longer.

## Root Cause
Tests run actual `pt-core agent plan` commands against the real system:
- Scans 1200+ real processes via /proc  
- Runs inference on ~936 processes after protected filter
- Each process: evidence building + posterior computation + decision making
- Cumulative time exceeds 120s timeout

## Evidence
- 11 tests failing with timeout
- stderr shows: 'event: inference_progress' at process counts up to 936
- Real inference appears to take ~1.2s per process (possibly debug build overhead)

## Proposed Solutions
1. **Mock scan results** - Use pre-recorded process snapshots as fixtures
2. **Sampling** - Run inference on random subset during tests  
3. **Test fixtures** - Create minimal process lists with known posteriors
4. **Test-specific fast-path** - Add flag to skip expensive supervision detection
5. **Parallelization** - Make inference loop concurrent (rayon)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] automation_mode tests pass reliably within 120s timeout
- [ ] Test coverage still validates automation mode behavior
- [ ] Solution doesn't mask real performance issues

## Notes
- Do NOT just bump the timeout - that masks performance issues
- StormyDog offered to help wire fast-path or sampling hook","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"StormyDog","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T14:05:25.618578426Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-17T04:01:54.143848435Z","closed_at":"2026-01-17T04:01:54.143848435Z","close_reason":"Added --sample-size flag to limit inference to N random processes.  {"id":"process_triage-bg5","title":"Create policy.json schema for loss matrix and guardrails","description":"## Task
Define the JSON schema for policy.json which contains the decision-theoretic loss matrix and safety guardrails.

## Background
Section 5.1 specifies the loss matrix:
- useful: keep=0, kill=100
- useful-but-bad: keep=10, kill=20
- abandoned: keep=30, kill=1
- zombie: keep=50, kill=1

But policy.json is broader - it defines:
1. Loss matrix for all actions (keep, pause, throttle, kill)
2. Safety guardrails (protected processes, rate limits)
3. Robot mode gates (min posterior, max blast radius, etc.)
4. FDR/alpha-investing parameters
5. Data-loss gate rules (open write handles)

## Deliverables
- JSON Schema for policy.json
- Default policy with conservative values
- Loss matrix specification
- Guardrail specification (protected patterns, denylists)
- Robot mode parameters specification
- FDR control parameters

## Technical Considerations
- Loss values should be interpretable (not arbitrary magic numbers)
- Guardrails must be regex-capable for process matching
- Robot mode gates must be independently configurable
- Support policy inheritance (org policy + user overrides)

## Example Structure
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "loss_matrix": {
    "useful": {"keep": 0, "pause": 5, "throttle": 8, "kill": 100},
    ...
  },
  "guardrails": {
    "protected_patterns": ["systemd", "sshd", "init", ...],
    "never_kill_ppid": [1],
    "max_kills_per_run": 10
  },
  "robot_mode": {
    "min_posterior": 0.95,
    "max_blast_radius_mb": 4096,
    "require_known_signature": false,
    "fdr_alpha": 0.05
  }
}

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-bgd","title":"Expand BATS test coverage for bash wrapper (pt)","description":"## Overview
Expand the **BATS** test suite for the existing bash `pt` wrapper so the current shipped tool remains safe and regression-resistant while `pt-core` is built.

This bead is explicitly about the **bash-first** implementation (AGENTS.md) and its heuristic/score-based behavior today.

## Why This Matters
Even after `pt-core` exists, the wrapper will still handle:
- install/update/tooling discovery
- launching `pt-core`
- graceful degradation paths

A strong wrapper test suite prevents shipping a broken “entrypoint experience”.

## Scope
### 1) Unit-ish tests via function sourcing
- Test pure functions by sourcing the script with mocks.

### 2) Integration tests with mock command injection
- Use PATH injection to mock `ps`, `pgrep`, `kill`, `who`, `ss`, etc.
- Validate that scan output and filtering behave deterministically.

### 3) E2E wrapper workflows
- `pt --help`, `pt scan`, `pt deep` (where supported), `pt history`, `pt clear`, `pt update`.
- Ensure non-interactive behavior does not hang (stdin closed).

## Test Categories (Examples)
### Scoring / classification behavior (current tool)
- Age / lifetime thresholds
- Orphan detection heuristics
- Protected process handling
- Decision memory adjustments

### Pattern normalization
- PID removal
- port normalization
- UUID normalization

### Decision memory
- JSON persistence validity
- read/write paths (XDG / config dir)

## Logging Requirements
- Tests should capture stderr and assert key log lines are present.
- When PT_DEBUG=1, tests should assert richer diagnostics exist (without flakiness).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Wrapper behavior has meaningful coverage beyond basic CLI smoke tests.
- [ ] Mock injection patterns are standardized (shared helpers).
- [ ] CI can run the suite reliably on Linux and macOS (skipping tests that truly require platform-only tools).

## Test Plan
- Unit: function-level tests for normalization and decision memory helpers.
- Integration: mock `ps` + `kill` flows to validate candidate selection and safety protections.
- E2E: scripted `pt` command invocations validating exit codes, non-hanging behavior, and stable outputs.
